query: FDA approval status of ivosidenib for treatment of acute myeloid leukemia

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Myeloid Leukemia -- Treatment / Management -- Relapsed/Post-HSCT Acute Myeloid Leukemia
retrieved_docs.content: Several agents are available for patients experiencing relapsed AML with specific mutations identified through molecular sequencing techniques. Fms-like tyrosine kinase 3 (FLT3) inhibitors, such as gilteritinib, may be recommended and have demonstrated higher complete remission rates than salvage chemotherapy in this patient population. [29] Patients with IDH1 mutations, either in the relapsed setting or among older populations, unfit individuals unsuitable for induction therapy, should be offered ivosidenib or olutasidenib. [30] [31] Similarly, individuals with IDH2 mutations under the same designation may be offered enasidenib. [32] Sorfaneib is approved as maintenance therapy following allogeneic HSCT for patients with FLT3-ITD mutations. [33]

retrieved_docs.title: Pharmacogenomics Overview -- Clinical Significance -- Genes and Broad-Spectrum Drug Approvals
retrieved_docs.content: Other classes of drugs still have the potential to join the list of tumor-agnostic approvals. Isocitrate dehydrogenase (IDH) 1 and IDH2 mutations are common in cancers such as gallbladder cancer, gliomas, and acute myeloid leukemia, leading to the accumulation of 2-HG, which drives tumorigenesis. Targeted inhibitors, such as ivosidenib and enasidenib, reduce 2-HG levels, thereby restoring normal function. Vorasidenib, a newly FDA-approved IDH inhibitor, shows benefits for some low-grade gliomas in the human brain. Olutasidenib, a selective IDH1 inhibitor, has demonstrated strong remission rates in patients with relapsed or refractory acute myeloid leukemia, resulting in FDA approval. As research advances, IDH-targeted therapies continue to show promise in improving patient outcomes. [60] [ National Cancer Institute. Vorasidenib Treatment Shows Promise for Some Low-Grade Gliomas ]

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Acute Leukemia -- Clinical Significance -- Treatment Strategies Based on Genetic Mutations
retrieved_docs.content: IDH1 and IDH2 mutations in acute myeloid leukemia: Patients with AML who have IDH1 or IDH2 mutations can be treated with specific inhibitors, such as ivosidenib or olutasidenib for IDH1 mutations and enasidenib for IDH2 mutations. These targeted therapies are used in cases where patients are refractory to initial treatment or experience relapse after standard therapy. [77]

retrieved_docs.title: Ixazomib -- Indications -- FDA Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Ixazomib is currently FDA-approved to treat relapsed or refractory multiple myeloma.

retrieved_docs.title: Filgrastim -- Indications -- FDA Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Minimizing the time to neutrophil recovery and the duration of fever following induction or consolidation chemotherapy treatment of adults with AML. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Myelogenous Leukemia -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: There are 4 United States Food and Drug Administration (FDA)-approved, first-line treatments for chronic phase CML that are commercially available tyrosine kinase inhibitors, including first-generation imatinib and second-generation dasatinib, nilotinib, and bosutinib.

retrieved_docs.title: Leukemia Cutis -- Medical Oncology
retrieved_docs.content: Initial treatment of myeloid leukemia is based on age, history of prior myelodysplastic syndrome or cytotoxic chemotherapy, and performance status. The use of cytogenetics and molecular markers gained through next-generation sequencing is necessary for risk stratification and can guide upfront frontline therapy. Several molecular markers such as FLT3, NPM1, CEBPA, IDH, And other mutations are important for risk assessment. However, although karyotyping and molecular markers are robust predictors of disease-free survival, induction chemotherapy is often initiated before this information is available. Standard induction regimens have as their basis the backbone of cytarabine in combination with anthracycline popularly known as the 7+3 regimen. The therapy intends to reduce the leukemic burden and restore normal hematopoiesis. For people that are found to be FLT3 mutated, midostaurin, an FLT3 inhibitor to both ITD and TKD mutations, is now added to standard chemotherapy as part of frontline treatment based on the RATIFY Alliance Trial.

retrieved_docs.title: Tyrosine Kinase Inhibitors -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: As of now, there are over 50 FDA-approved TKIs. Comprehensive lists of FDA-approved TKIs with additional information are available at NIH PubChem and FDA.gov. Due to the broad reach of this topic and the rapid development of new drugs, this list is not fully comprehensive.

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Acute Leukemia -- Clinical Significance -- Treatment Strategies Based on Genetic Mutations
retrieved_docs.content: FLT3 mutations in acute myeloid leukemia: Patients with AML who have FLT3-ITD (internal tandem duplication) mutations are at an increased risk of relapse and have a poorer prognosis. Targeted therapies, including FLT3 inhibitors such as midostaurin, quizartinib, and gilteritinib, are integrated into treatment regimens to improve outcomes. These inhibitors are used in combination with standard chemotherapy during the induction and consolidation phases and may continue as maintenance therapy. [74]

retrieved_docs.title: Biosimilars Use in Medicine for Inflammatory Diseases -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Other indications of biosimilars alongside inflammatory diseases include management for specific malignancies and secondary causes of anemia. In September 2017, bevacizumab-awwb was the first biosimilar that was granted FDA approval for certain cancers. A few months later, in December 2017, biosimilar trastuzumab-dkst was also granted FDA approval for specific malignancies. Cancers indicative of biosimilar therapy include non-squamous non-small cell lung cancer, metastatic colorectal cancer, metastatic renal cell carcinoma, glioblastoma, recurrent or metastatic cervical cancer, HER2 (human epidermal growth factor receptor 2) associated breast cancer, and HER2 associated gastric (metastatic) or gastroesophageal junction adenocarcinoma, chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL), non-Hodgkin lymphoma (NHL). To date, new biosimilars are currently still being FDA approved, with the latest approval coming on July 28, 2021, for biosimilar agent insulin glargine-yfgn, a long-acting insulin agent indicated for pediatric type 1 diabetes mellitus (DM) and adult patients with DM type 1 and DM type 2.

retrieved_docs.title: Filgrastim -- Indications -- FDA Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Reduction of the incidence of infection manifested by febrile neutropenia in patients receiving myelosuppressive chemotherapy. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Filgrastim -- Indications -- FDA Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Shortening the duration of neutropenia and neutropenia-related clinical sequelae in patients with non-myeloid malignancies undergoing myeloablative chemotherapy followed by marrow transplantation. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Daunorubicin -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: In 2017 the liposomal co-formulation of daunorubicin and cytarabine was approved by the FDA for the treatment of adults with therapy-related AML or AML with myelodysplasia-related changes (AML-MRC), these two types of AML are known for their poor prognosis. [5] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Vemurafenib -- Indications -- Off-Label Uses
retrieved_docs.content: In addition to its FDA-approved indications, vemurafenib has been used off-label in the treatment of several other BRAF-mutated malignancies, including: Metastatic and unresectable melanoma with BRAF V600K mutation [16] Refractory non-small cell lung carcinoma BRAF V600 mutation [17] BRAF V600E-positive refractory metastatic or unresectable papillary thyroid cancer [18] Refractory hairy cell leukemia [19] [20]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2021
retrieved_docs.content: Gilteritinib is a targeted therapeutic agent that may be used to treat acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد), offering a potential option for patients with this aggressive hematologic malignancy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 214
retrieved_docs.content: The community describes blood cancer (ابْيضَاضُ الدَّم) as a group of leukemic disorders comprising lymphoid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي) and myeloid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم النقوي), which correspond to chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن) and acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد), respectively. Targeted therapy relies heavily on protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents. BCR‑ABL inhibitors—dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, imatinib mesylát, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib—block the BCR‑ABL tyrosine kinase; the former four are used for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia. Bruton's tyrosine kinase inhibitors ibrutinib and zanubrutinib also belong to this class, with ibrutinib approved for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Additional protein kinase inhibitors include EGFR inhibitors canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, as well as other ATC‑listed agents cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin; gilteritinib and midostaurin are employed in acute myeloid leukemia. Corticosteroids such as dexamethasone acetate (used for blood cancer and septic shock), dexamethasone phosphate, prednisolone sodium metasulphobenzoate, prednisolone sodium phosphate, and prednisolone tebutate demonstrate efficacy in hematologic malignancies. Chemotherapeutic agents irinotecan hydrochloride and pipobroman are also utilized. Contraindicated interventions include infection with human herpesvirus 1 and oncolytic viral therapy talimogene laherparepvec, which may exacerbate the disease. Collectively, these agents illustrate the role of kinase‑targeted and immunomodulatory drugs in the comprehensive management of leukemic disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 326
retrieved_docs.content: Ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib are therapeutic agents that may be used to treat chronic myeloid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم النقوي). Both ponatinib and its hydrochloride salt share the same indication, reflecting that the active compound is effective in this hematologic malignancy. Asciminib, a distinct BCR‑ABL1 inhibitor, also offers a treatment option for patients with chronic myeloid leukemia, particularly those with resistance to earlier tyrosine‑kinase inhibitors. The availability of multiple agents targeting the same disease provides clinicians with alternative strategies to achieve disease control and improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 214
retrieved_docs.content: Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors are classified as protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity. Within this class, ibrutinib and zanubrutinib are members; ibrutinib is used to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن). Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors also belong to the broader category of protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents. Canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab are specific EGFR inhibitors. Other protein kinase inhibitors listed in the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) classification include cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin. Gilteritinib and midostaurin are indicated for the treatment of acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد), while cabozantinib is a member of this group with oncologic applications. Collectively, these agents illustrate how diverse protein kinase–targeting drugs—spanning BTK inhibitors, EGFR inhibitors, and other kinase inhibitors—are integrated into cancer therapy, each targeting specific malignancies and contributing to personalized patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 81
retrieved_docs.content: Blood cancer (ابْيضَاضُ الدَّم) encompasses leukemias, including lymphoid (ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي) and myeloid (ابيضاض الدم النقوي) forms, with specific subtypes such as chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن) and acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد). Targeted kinase inhibition is a central therapeutic strategy. BCR‑ABL inhibitors—dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib—are employed, the first three for lymphoid leukemia and the latter three for myeloid leukemia. BTK inhibitors, namely ibrutinib and zanubrutinib, belong to the same protein‑kinase inhibitor class, with ibrutinib indicated for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Additional protein‑kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity include EGFR inhibitors canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, as well as cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin; gilteritinib and midostaurin are specifically used in acute myeloid leukemia. Conventional cytotoxic and corticosteroid agents such as cytarabine, dexamethasone acetate, dexamethasone phosphate, dexamethasonisonikotinát, irinotecan hydrochloride, pipobroman, and prednisolone sodium metasulphobenzoate, prednisolone sodium phosphate, and prednisolone tebutate also have activity against various blood cancers. Dexamethasone acetate additionally serves in the management of septic shock. Conversely, viral agents such as human herpesvirus‑1 and the oncolytic therapy talimogene laherparepvec are contraindicated in patients with blood cancer. These interrelated disease classifications, drug classes, and contraindications illustrate the complexity of therapeutic decision‑making in hematologic malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1768
retrieved_docs.content: BCR-ABL Inhibitor is a class of targeted therapy that includes ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib. Ponatinib and its hydrochloride form, as well as asciminib, are members of the BCR-ABL Inhibitor class and are employed in the treatment of أبيضاض الدم النقوي, a myeloid leukemia. These agents act by inhibiting the BCR‑ABL tyrosine kinase, thereby controlling disease progression. Their shared mechanism and therapeutic indication underscore the importance of BCR‑ABL inhibition in managing this hematologic malignancy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 329
retrieved_docs.content: The ATC category “Other protein kinase inhibitors in ATC” comprises the kinase‑targeted agents cabozantinib, gilteritinib and midostaurin. Both gilteritinib and midostaurin are indicated as potential therapies for acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد). As members of the same protein‑kinase inhibitor class, these drugs share a common mechanism of action that involves inhibition of specific signaling pathways driving malignant cell proliferation, and their use in AML highlights the therapeutic relevance of kinase inhibition in hematologic malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 48
retrieved_docs.content: BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a subgroup of protein kinase inhibitors classified as antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents, target the BCR‑ABL fusion protein. Dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are BCR‑ABL inhibitors indicated for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are BCR‑ABL inhibitors used for myeloid leukemia. Bruton’s tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors, also protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity, include ibrutinib and zanubrutinib; ibrutinib is approved for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors, another subclass of protein kinase inhibitors, comprise canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, both belonging to the same antineoplastic and immunomodulating class. Cabozantinib, gilteritinib and midostaurin are members of the ATC subgroup “Other protein kinase inhibitors,” also classified as antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents, and gilteritinib and midostaurin have activity against acute myeloid leukemia. Collectively, these agents illustrate how precise molecular targeting of BCR‑ABL, BTK, EGFR and other kinases provides distinct therapeutic options across lymphoid, myeloid and chronic lymphocytic leukemias, underscoring the role of targeted kinase inhibition in modern oncology care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 98
retrieved_docs.content: The community’s knowledge base defines leukemia (ابْيضَاضُ الدَّم) as a hematologic malignancy comprising lymphoid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي) and myeloid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم النقوي). BCR‑ABL inhibitors—dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib—share the mechanism of inhibiting the BCR‑ABL tyrosine‑kinase fusion protein and are assigned to specific subtypes: dasatinib, its anhydrous form, and imatinib are indicated for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia. Additional anti‑leukemic agents include the corticosteroid derivative kortizon acetát, dexamethasone phosphate, the topoisomerase‑I inhibitor irinotecan hydrochloride, the antimetabolite methotrexate sodium, the alkylating agent pipobroman, and several prednisolone salts (prednisolone sodium metasulphobenzoate, prednisolone sodium phosphate, prednisolone tebutate). Conversely, the oncolytic viral therapy talimogene laherparepvec is contraindicated in patients with any form of leukemia. These relationships support precise drug selection and comprehensive management of hematologic oncology patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2646
retrieved_docs.content: Dasatinib and dasatinib anhydrous are both classified as BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a class of targeted therapies that block the BCR‑ABL tyrosine kinase activity implicated in certain leukemias. By inhibiting this oncogenic kinase, both the standard and anhydrous formulations of dasatinib help to control malignant cell proliferation and are used in the management of chronic myeloid leukemia and Philadelphia chromosome‑positive acute lymphoblastic leukemia. Recognizing dasatinib and its anhydrous form as BCR‑ABL inhibitors underscores their shared mechanism of action and therapeutic role in precision oncology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1291
retrieved_docs.content: Midostaurin is a therapeutic agent that may be used to treat acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 88
retrieved_docs.content: BCR‑ABL inhibitors—including dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib—are classified as protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents; dasatinib, its anhydrous form and imatinib are indicated for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are used for myeloid leukemia. Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors such as ibrutinib and zanubrutinib also belong to the protein kinase inhibitor, antineoplastic and immunomodulating group, with ibrutinib specifically indicated for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors, exemplified by canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, are another subset of protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents. Additional protein kinase inhibitors in this ATC subclass include cabozantinib, gilteritinib and midostaurin; gilteritinib and midostaurin may be employed in the treatment of acute myeloid leukemia. Collectively, these kinase‑targeted therapies illustrate how distinct protein kinase inhibitor subclasses are organized within broader antineoplastic categories and matched to specific hematologic and lymphoid malignancies, supporting precise, patient‑centered treatment strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 1 -- Community 96
retrieved_docs.content: specifically used for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Additional protein‑kinase inhibitors used in acute myeloid leukemia include gilteritinib, midostaurin, and cabozantinib. EGFR inhibitors such as canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab are also noted, reflecting the broader category of kinase‑targeted agents with antineoplastic and immunomodulating properties. Conventional agents employed in blood‑cancer therapy comprise corticosteroids (cortisone acetate, dexamethasone acetate, dexamethasone phosphate, prednisolone sodium metasulphobenzoate, prednisolone sodium phosphate, prednisolone tebutate), antimetabolites and alkylating agents (cytarabine, irinotecan hydrochloride, pipobroman). Cortisone acetate is highlighted as the active corticosteroid component of an oral liquid botanical formulation used in supportive care, and dexamethasone acetate is also indicated for septic shock (صدمة إنتانيّة). retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 4 -- Community 2916
retrieved_docs.content: Dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are both classified as BCR‑ABL inhibitors. As BCR‑ABL inhibitors, these agents specifically target the BCR‑ABL fusion protein, a constitutively active tyrosine kinase that drives the development of chronic myeloid leukemia and certain acute lymphoblastic leukemias. By inhibiting BCR‑ABL activity, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib disrupt downstream signaling pathways, reducing malignant cell proliferation and providing therapeutic benefit in BCR‑ABL–positive hematologic malignancies. Their shared classification highlights a common mechanism of action, while differences in potency, spectrum of activity, and side‑effect profiles inform clinicians’ choices of targeted therapy for patients with BCR‑ABL–driven disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 70
retrieved_docs.content: BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating properties, include dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib. Dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are indicated for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia. Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors, also protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity, comprise ibrutinib and zanubrutinib; ibrutinib is used to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors, another protein kinase inhibitor subclass, consist of canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab. By blocking EGFR tyrosine‑kinase signaling, these agents disrupt tumor cell proliferation and survival and can modulate immune responses, providing antineoplastic and immunomodulating effects. Collectively, targeted inhibition of BCR‑ABL, BTK, and EGFR exemplifies precision oncology, linking specific molecular targets to distinct hematologic and solid malignancies and guiding patient‑centered cancer therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 1 -- Community 96
retrieved_docs.content: The community describes a comprehensive pharmacologic landscape for blood cancers (ابْيضَاضُ الدَّم), particularly leukemias such as lymphoid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي), myeloid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم النقوي), chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن) and acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد). BCR‑ABL inhibitors—dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib—are classified as protein‑kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity; the first three are indicated for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia. Bruton’s tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors, ibrutinib and zanubrutinib, also belong to this class, with ibrutinib specifically used for chronic lymphocytic leukemia.
